data.title: Manazel Eyes Expansion Into Hospitality Sector
data.description:
Manazel (ADX:MANAZEL) plans to expand into the hospitality sector after a return to profitability.

The real estate company's hospitality portfolio will be based on its 2,000 hotel apartments, according to a Thursday release.

NTT Docomo 4Q Mobile Average Monthly Revenue Per User Y3,950 Vs Y3,930 a Year Earlier

Bioretec CEO to Step Down
data.description:
Bioretec Chief Executive Officer Alan Donze plans to resign and step down by July 7.

The Finnish medical device company said in a Thursday release that its board will immediately commence the recruitment process for a successor.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Financial Services News of the Day - Friday at 12 AM ET
data.description:
Macquarie to Wait Out Volatility Before Divesting More Renewables

The Australian financial giant is aiming to wait out current market volatility before divesting any more renewable-energy assets.

Coinbase Strikes $2.9 Billion Deal for Major Crypto Options Platform

The company agreed to acquire Deribit, a push into the highly profitable crypto derivatives market and a move meant to bolster its global growth.

Bank of England Cuts Rates After Fed Stands Pat

The Bank of England cut rates, highlighting a growing gap in how global central banks and the Federal Reserve are approaching Trump's tariffs.

Carlyle Looks to Europe for Private-Credit Expansion

Less competition for deals and fundraising makes the Continent more attractive as lenders crowd the U.S. market.

Car Insurance Rates Were Ready to Drop. Then Tariffs Came Along.

Insurers have slowed requests to regulators for rate decreases.

Bank of Canada's No. 2 Official Sounds Alarm on Hedge Fund Holdings of Government Debt

Carolyn Rogers urges hedge funds with sizable Canadian sovereign debt to sharpen risk-management, liquidity strategies.

Coinbase Reports Drop in Profit

Earlier in the day Coinbase announced a $2.9 billion cash-and-stock deal to acquire Deribit, a bitcoin options exchange.

Celsius's Mashinsky, Former Crypto High-Roller, Gets 12-Year Prison Sentence

Federal prosecutors had sought a sentence of at least 20 years for Alex Mashinsky, who attracted customers-many of them ordinary investors-by urging them to "unbank" themselves and park their savings at Celsius.

Why the Fed Isn't Ready to Join Other Central Banks in Cutting Rates

The U.S. faces risks of higher inflation and unemployment. Countries without tax hikes on imports are focused on the hit to growth.

One of Warren Buffett's Last Big Bets Is Souring

Berkshire Hathaway has amassed 28% of Occidental Petroleum, but its stock has slumped as U.S. oil prices come under pressure.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Energy News of the Day - Friday at 12 AM ET
data.description:
Oil Futures Rally on U.S.-U.K. Trade Deal Optimism

Oil futures rallied on news of a trade deal between the U.S. and the U.K. that includes lowering the tariff on British-made cars imported into the U.S. and exempting some goods from an overall 10% tariff.

U.S. Natural Gas Futures Slip as Storage Builds

Natural gas futures lost a few cents in choppy trade as a weekly storage report confirmed an expected increase in the surplus over the five-year average.

Conoco Cuts CapEx View on Volatile Economic Status

The Houston oil producer posted first-quarter earnings of $2.8 billion, or $2.23 a share, up from $2.6 billion, or $2.15 a share, a year earlier.

Siemens Energy Expects Limited Hit From U.S. Tariffs

The maker of energy equipment reported a surge in profit and anticipates limited direct impact from U.S. tariffs in its fiscal second half.

Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on Ithaca Energy, crude futures, Centrica and more in the latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities.

Trump Says He Hasn't Decided if Iran Can Enrich Uranium in a New Deal

The president's latest comments add to the administration's mixed messages as it pursues talks for a new nuclear pact with Tehran.

Syria Dangles Oil, Peace for U.S. Help on Sanctions

The former Islamist rebels who run the new government are looking to convince a skeptical Trump administration

U.S. Crude Oil Stockpiles Fall Amid Lower Production

U.S. crude oil inventories fell by 2 million barrels to 438.4 million barrels in the week ended May 2 and were about 7% below the five-year average for the time of year, the EIA said.

Orsted Warns of Earnings Hit After Axing U.K. Offshore Wind Project

The Danish company won't continue the Hornsea 4 offshore wind project in the U.K. due to rising costs.

Veolia to Buy CDPQ's Stake in Water Technologies and Solutions for $1.75 Billion

The deal means Veolia takes full ownership of Caisse de depot et placement du Quebec's Water Technologies and Solutions subsidiary.

data.title: NTT 5G Service Subscribers 37.3M at End-March Vs 29.7M a Year Earlier
Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0242 GMT - Palm oil prices are higher in Asian trade, tracking overnight gains in soybean oil on the Chicago Board of Trade and higher crude-oil prices, AmInvestment Bank says in a note. Stronger crude oil prices will support CPO prices, as it encourage palm oil's appeal as a biofuel alternative. Technical analysis suggests CPO futures turned neutral or slightly bullish, with AmInvestment Bank seeing support at 3,747 ringgit a ton and resistance at 3,894 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for July delivery is higher by 32 ringgit to 3,833 ringgit a ton. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0232 GMT - Iron ore prices fall as China looks to curb steel output, ANZ Research analysts write in a note. The China Iron and Steel Association say the government is actively deploying and promoting its crude steel production mandate. While no specific targets are released, there is market speculation that as much as 50 million tonnes of output a year could be curtailed, they add. Annual production remains stubbornly above 1 billion tonnes, they say. The most-traded iron ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is down 0.4% at CNY697.00 a ton. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0154 GMT - Gold trading has likely found a new baseline "floor price" around $3,000-$3,100 an ounce, State Street Global Advisors' SPDR gold strategy team says in a note. Its base-case scenario is for gold to trade at $3,100-$3,500 an ounce in 2025. Under this scenario, "harsher trade and tariff rates are rolled back, including between U.S.-China, but policy uncertainty and tensions remain on the front foot for the balance of 2025," the team says. Other factors under this scenario include a Fed that is limited in its ability to lower rates owing to inflation uncertainty even as U.S. and global growth slows. Spot gold edges 0.1% lower to $3,301.57 an ounce. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0119 GMT - Copper edges higher in early Asian trade amid hopes for U.S.-China trade talks. President Trump sounded an optimistic note about U.S.-China talks scheduled for this weekend in Switzerland and said he could possibly talk with Chinese leader Xi Jinping after the negotiations. Traders are also more appreciative of China's recent stimulus measures, which include cutting the required reserve ratio and mortgage rates, ANZ Research analysts say in a note. This should provide financial support to small- and medium-sized enterprises as well as tariff-hit firms, they add. The three-month LME copper contract is 0.1% higher at $9,439.50 a ton.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

2341 GMT - Gold rises in the early Asian session on a possible technical recovery after front-month Comex gold futures settled 2.5% lower overnight. However, gains may be capped by risk-on mood. There's renewed optimism over trade talks, beginning with the U.S.-U.K. deal, says Fawad Razaqzada, market analyst at City Index and FOREX.com, in an email. President Trump also suggested that U.S. tariffs on imports from China could be lowered if talks with China progress well, Razaqzada says. Should risk sentiment improve further, there could be more pressure on gold price, the market analyst adds. Spot gold is 0.3% higher at $3,315.41/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2257 GMT [Dow Jones]--Near term, North American lumber markets are expected to remain volatile, Interfor said, but added mid-term, lumber from Canada is expected to remain a key source of supply to meet U.S. needs. Interfor also said near-term volatility could be affected by potential tariffs on Canadian lumber exports to the U.S. Growth in U.S. lumber manufacturing capacity may be limited by labor constraints, lengthy equipment lead times, and other factors, Interfor said, adding it "expects that over the mid-term, lumber markets will continue to benefit from favorable underlying supply and demand fundamentals." (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

2101 GMT - Tyson Foods named its former CFO and great-grandson of its founder, John Randal Tyson, and his sister to its board of directors. Tyson stepped down as CFO of the company last year after his second alcohol-related arrest while in the role. He had been on leave from the company. His sister, Olivia Tyson, has run the family's charitable foundation since 2018. Both have been involved in board meetings for years. Their father, John H. Tyson, is chairman of the company, and the family maintains about 70% of Tyson's voting power. "I am confident about our strategy and excited to step into a new chapter of working with the fourth generation of Tyson family members to help oversee the business," Tyson said in a statement. ( patrick.thomas@wsj.com )

1934 GMT - Live cattle futures rise 0.8% to a record $2.14125 a pound after the confirmation of a pending trade deal between the U.S. and U.K. U.S. beef is said to be one category of export goods that the U.K. will procure more of under the pact. But traders question how much upside cattle futures have left, if any. "This market… is pricing far beyond any measure of economic value," says Rich Nelson of Allendale Inc., who calls current price levels "significantly overpriced." Lean hogs close down 0.1% to 97.275 cents a pound on the CME. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1853 GMT - Oil futures rally on news of a trade deal between the U.S. and the U.K. that includes lowering the tariff on British-made cars imported into the U.S. and exempting some goods from an overall 10% tariff. "Crude oil and the broader market are obviously looking at the administration's first deal as a potential demand-growth event," says Mizuho's Robert Yawger in a note. Strength in gasoline, with refining margins rising, is also supportive of crude, he adds. "Gasoline stronger than crude oil is always good fundamentals, and underpins refiner demand for feedstock crude oil used to make petroleum products." WTI settles up 3.2% at $59.91 a barrel and Brent rises 2.8% to $62.84 a barrel.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1846 GMT - Natural gas futures lose a few cents in choppy trade as a weekly storage report confirms an expected increase in the surplus over the five-year average. Natural gas inventories increased by 104 Bcf last week to 2,145 Bcf, and were 30 Bcf above the 2020-2024 average for the week, the EIA reports. The injection was slightly larger than the 101 Bcf in a Wall Street Journal survey of analysts. "The rate of injection is expected to remain robust in the coming weeks as mild temperatures will help limit natural gas demand," Andy Huenefeld of Pinebrook Energy Advisors says in a note. Nymex natural gas settles down 0.8% at $3.592/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1736 GMT - Fund traders are seen as moving out of holding longs on corn, this even though over 1.6 million metric tons of U.S. corn exports were announced as being sold in today's report from the USDA--a bigger number than expected by analysts. "In spite of awesome export news, funds are being relentless with selling, seeming to think there will be perfect weather this summer," says Naomi Blohm of Total Farm Marketing. Even with corn-based ethanol getting a nod as potential top export to the UK, traders are not sure the benefits will hit corn prices immediately, or if at all. Corn is down 1.1%, and wheat is off 1.2%, while soybeans rise 0.1%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1635 GMT - The U.S.-UK trade deal is expected to be a boon for U.S. beef demand. In a statement after President Trump's announcement, the National Cattlemen's Beef Association called the U.K. "an ideal partner for trade." The NCBA says that the U.K. became a big opportunity for American beef suppliers after the country left the EU, as U.K. consumers are big meat consumers. In the first two months of 2025 the U.K. imported roughly 47,000 metric tons of beef — with over 30,000 tons coming from Ireland, according to data from U.K.'s Agriculture and Horticulture Development Board. In that timeframe, the U.K. has only committed to purchase just over 1,000 tons of U.S. beef, according to USDA data. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Costa Rica GDP Growth Slows to 3.9% in Q1 2025
data.description:
Costa Rica’s GDP grew by 3.9% year-on-year in the first quarter of 2025, slowing from a 4.3% expansion in the preceding quarter.

Growth accelerated in education and human health and social care (2.3% vs 2.0% in Q4 2024) and wholesale & retail trade (2.8% vs. 2.7%), while it rebounded for electricity, water & sanitation services (1.4% vs -2.4%).

Conversely, production moderated in manufacturing (5.8% vs 6.3%), construction (2.8% vs 4.4%), transport and storage (5.4% vs 7.2%), accommodation and food services activities (0.1% vs 1.4%), information and communications (4.2% vs 5.5%), real estate (2.6% vs 3.4%), and financial and insurance activities (5.7% vs 5.8%).

Meanwhile, the mining and quarrying sector contracted (-2.3% vs. 0.5%).

On a seasonally adjusted quarterly basis, the economy grew by 0.2%, easing from 1.6% in the previous period.

data.title: EMEA Morning Briefing: Trade Talks Optimism Buoys Mood
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Watch For:

Trading updates from Commerzbank, IAG, AngloGold Ashanti, EDP, Sibanye-Stillwater

Opening Call:

European stock futures advanced early Friday as an initial U.S.-U.K. trade agreement boosted sentiment. Asian stock benchmarks rose; the dollar and Treasury yields steadied; while oil and gold futures gained.

Equities:

Stock futures pointed to gains at Friday's open amid optimism stemming from an improving trade relationship with the United Kingdom and hopes for talks with China.

The U.K. deal is the first of its kind since the Trump administration unleashed broad trade restrictions five weeks ago, sparking market turmoil. Stocks have since climbed at any sign of de-escalation, with many investors hoping the administration can reach deals that will minimize the blow to economic growth and corporate profits.

However, the limited details offered have left some analysts underwhelmed. "Along with the push to get trade talks with China underway too this weekend, this rush to demonstrate progress on 'deals' reveals a rising desperation within the administration to rollback tariffs before they hit GDP growth and inflation," said Paul Ashworth of Capital Economics.

Ashworth added that "even the broad details still appear to be vague," although comments from President Trump suggest that a 10% baseline tariff looks to remain in place.

Forex:

EUR/USD may undergo a deeper corrective pullback after its break below a key daily support zone, said UOB's Quek Ser Leang. According to the daily chart, the currency pair dropped below the support zone of between 1.1215 and 1.1225 on Thursday, suggesting the recent high of 1.1573 could be an interim top, he said.

The pullback could extend toward the 55-day exponential moving average, which is currently at 1.1050. On the upside, 1.1400 is a strong near-term resistance level, he added.

Bonds:

U.K. long-dated gilts look more appealing than their U.S. peers as the U.K.'s term premium better reflects analyst expectations of where interest rates will settle in the long-term, said Vivek Paul, U.K. chief investment strategist at BlackRock Investment Institute.

This is expected to rise globally but the U.K. term premium looks favorable relative to the U.S. term premium, Paul said.

Energy:

Oil futures rose slightly amid optimism over U.S.-China trade talks. Trump sounded an optimistic note the talks scheduled for this weekend in Switzerland, and said he could potentially speak with Chinese President Xi following the negotiations.

"Talks between the world's two largest economies aim to ease trade tensions that have significantly impacted the global economy, particularly the energy market," XS.com's Antonio Di Giacomo said.

"Investors hope that progress in these negotiations will help stabilize global crude oil demand," he added.

Metals:

Gold gained in Asia after front-month Comex gold futures settled 2.5% lower overnight. However, gains may be capped by risk-on mood. There's renewed optimism over trade talks, beginning with the U.S.-U.K. deal, said Fawad Razaqzada, market analyst at City Index and FOREX.com.

Trump also suggested that U.S. tariffs on imports from China could be lowered if talks with China progress well, Razaqzada said. Should risk sentiment improve further, there could be more pressure on gold price, he added.

-

Copper edged higher amid hopes for U.S.-China trade talks. Trump has sounded an optimistic note about the talks. Traders are also more appreciative of China's recent stimulus measures, which include cutting the required reserve ratio and mortgage rates, ANZ said. This should provide financial support to small and medium-size enterprises as well as tariff-hit firms, ANZ added.

TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

China's Export Growth Remained Resilient in April

China's exports continued to expand at a solid pace in April, despite hefty U.S. tariffs on Chinese goods, official data showed Friday.

Outbound shipments rose 8.1% from a year earlier in April, after rising 12.4% in March, the General Administration of Customs said Friday. The result beat the 2.5% increase expected by economists in a Wall Street Journal poll.

Trump's Bespoke Trade Deal With U.K. Sets Little Precedent for Other Nations

WASHINGTON-President Trump made a deal. Now comes the hard part: getting more.

Trump agreed to a framework for a trade agreement with the U.K., giving his administration momentum as it faces pressure to notch scores more to avoid hurting American consumers. But the deal was limited in scope and included niche issues regarding the U.K., meaning it didn't offer other nations a clear road map to follow, foreign officials said.

With U.K. Deal, U.S. Signals That 10% Tariff on World Is New Baseline

If anyone deserved a good trade deal with the U.S., it was Britain. It actually runs a trade deficit with the U.S., buys a lot of manufactured U.S. products, spends plenty on its own defense, and has a leader who President Trump likes.

And yet the agreement announced Thursday was, for Britain, a bad deal, not a good deal. Its exports to the U.S. will now face a minimum tariff of 10%, up from less than 2% in 2023, with some exceptions, such as for steel and jet engines. Exports of autos above 100,000 units will face a 25% tariff. Britain will ease restrictions on U.S. beef and ethanol.

News Corp Revenue Boosted by Gains at Dow Jones, HarperCollins

News Corp reported revenue and profit gains for its fiscal third quarter, driven by growth at Dow Jones, HarperCollins Publishers and the digital real-estate group.

"We have pursued digital growth, realigned our assets, focused relentlessly on cost discipline and asserted the essential value of our intellectual property in a changing, challenging content world," said Chief Executive Robert Thomson.

Write to singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

Expected Major Events for Friday

04:30/NED: Mar Manufacturing output

05:00/FIN: Mar Industrial Production

06:00/DEN: Mar External trade (provisional figures)

All times in GMT. Powered by Onclusive and Dow Jones.

Write to us at newsletters@dowjones.com

We offer an enhanced version of this briefing that is optimized for viewing on mobile devices and sent directly to your email inbox. If you would like to sign up, please go to https://newsplus.wsj.com/subscriptions.

This article is a text version of a Wall Street Journal newsletter published earlier today.

data.title: Fujifilm Holdings Profit Climbs 7.2% in Fiscal 2024
data.description:
Fujifilm Holdings reported a 7.2% increase in profit attributable to owners of the parent to 260.95 billion yen for fiscal 2024, up from 243.51 billion yen a year ago.

The photography, optics and medical electronics company's earnings per share rose to 216.46 yen from 202.05 yen a year ago, according to its Tokyo bourse filing on Thursday.

Revenue increased 7.9% to 3.196 trillion yen in the fiscal year ended March 31, compared with 2.96 trillion yen a year earlier.

Fujifilm declared a year-end dividend of 35.00 yen per share for fiscal 2025, payable from June 30.

For fiscal 2025, the company expects attributable profit to rise 0.4% to 262.00 billion yen, or 217.46 yen per share, while net sales are forecast to grow 2.6% to 3.280 trillion yen.

The company plans to pay interim and year-end dividends of 35.00 yen per share, each, for fiscal 2025, totaling 70.00 yen per share for the year.

data.title: Oman & Emirates Investment to Halt Trading on Abu Dhabi Bourse
data.description:
Oman & Emirates Investment Holding Co. (ADX:OEIHC) shares will temporarily pause trading on the Abu Dhabi Securities Exchange from 12 pm local time on May 12 due to its board meeting.

The investment manager will resume trading after the meeting results are disclosed, according to a Thursday release.

data.title: Australia's ANZ falls; brokerages cut PT on weak outlook
data.description:
** ANZ falls 0.8% to A$29.16; set to snap three-week winning streak with ~4% weekly loss

** Stock underperforms financials' 1.2% rise

** Jefferies trims PT to A$25.95 from A$26.33

** Assumes 10% dividend cut from H2 FY25, with high payout ratio amid expense headwinds

** Morgan Stanley lowers PT by ~6% to A$27.50 on reducing cash EPS estimates for FY26 and FY27 by 1% and 4% respectively due to weaker revenue growth

** UBS cuts PT to A$30 from A$31 after reducing cash earnings estimates by 2.3%, 0.6% and 0.4% for FY25, FY26 and FY27, respectively

** ANZ reports flat H1 cash earnings with pressured margins, elevated gross impaired assets

** Stock gains 3% YTD

data.title: Iraq's SOMO offers fuel oil term supplies for second-half of 2025, sources say
data.description:
Iraq's state-owned marketer SOMO has offered a term contract to supply high-sulphur fuel oil between July and December 2025, according to industry sources.

The fuel oil will be lifted from the Khor al-Zubair (KAZ) ship-to-ship transfer port, with a volume of about 145,000 metric tons per month. The tender will close on May 18, with a validity of up to 20 days.

data.title: NTT 4Q Ebitda Margin 18.8x% Vs 23.1% a Year Earlier
Al Buhaira National Insurance to Halt Trading on Abu Dhabi Bourse
data.description:
Al Buhaira National Insurance (ADX:ABNIC) shares will temporarily pause trading on the Abu Dhabi Securities Exchange from 11 am local time on May 12 due to its board meeting.

The United Arab Emirates-based insurer will resume trading once its meeting results are published, according to a Thursday notice.

data.title: Malaysia Q1 Construction Output Rises the Least in A Year
data.description: Construction activity in Malaysia increased by 16.6% year-on-year in Q1 of 2025, slowing from a 23.1% surge in Q4 and marking the softest pace since Q1 of 2024.

data.title: DeSoto Resources Says Further Assays Extend Gold Anomaly in Guinea Project; Shares Up 7%
data.description:
DeSoto Resources said assay results from 31 rock chip and 287 dump samples from the Dadjan project in Guinea extended a gold-in-soil anomalism to 3.6 kilometers, according to a Friday filing with the Australian bourse.

Power auger drilling is underway, with further assay results pending, the filing said.

The company is currently executing early stage target generation across its 14 projects, according to the filing.

Shares rose nearly 7% in afternoon trade Friday.

data.title: CompuGroup Medical Receives Delisting Offer from CVC Capital
data.description:
CompuGroup Medical said Thursday it plans to delist following a public offer from private equity company CVC Capital Partners.

The delisting offer of 22 euros per share for the healthcare software company follows a voluntary public offer completed on May 2, through which CVC gained a 24.27% stake in CompuGroup.

The acceptance period for the delisting offer is expected to commence in May and the deal is expected to close in the first half or before CompuGroup's annual general meeting on Aug. 1.

CompuGroup's supervisory and administrative boards and managing directors plan to recommend that shareholders accept the delisting offer.

data.title: China's Trade Surplus Declines in April; Exports Top Forecasts
data.description:
China's trade surplus declined to $96.2 billion in April, versus $102.6 billion in March, according to data released by China's General Administration of Customs.

The recent figure was higher than the Reuters forecast of $89 billion in trade surplus.

In the first four months of 2025, the trade surplus stood at $368.8 billion.

Exports rose 8.1% to $315.7 billion in April, faster than the 1.9% growth expected in a Reuters poll. Total import value ticked down 0.2% to $219.5 billion, slower than the Reuters forecast of a 5.9% fall.

Total import and export value rose 4.6% year over year to $535.2 billion in April.

data.title: Cantaloupe Inc reports results for the quarter ended March 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• Cantaloupe Inc reported quarterly adjusted earnings of 65 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended March 31, higher than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of 6 cents. The mean expectation of five analysts for the quarter was for earnings of 10 cents per share. Wall Street expected results to range from 9 cents to 10 cents per share.

  • Revenue rose 11.1% to $75.43 million from a year ago; analysts expected $79.83 million.

  • Cantaloupe Inc's reported EPS for the quarter was 65 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $48.87 million.

  • Cantaloupe Inc shares had risen by 4.4% this quarter and lost 13.6% so far this year.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had risen by about 2.1% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no negative revisions of earnings estimates

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 6 "strong buy" or "buy," no "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the financial technology (fintech) peer group is also "buy"

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Cantaloupe Inc is $12.00
